rawText:
In Moulmein, in Lower Burma, I was hated by large numbers of people — the only time in my life that I have been important enough for this to happen to me. I was sub-divisional police officer of the town, and in an aimless, petty kind of way anti-European feeling was very bitter. No one had the guts to raise a riot, but if a European woman went through the bazaars alone somebody would probably spit betel juice over her dress. As a police officer I was an obvious target and was baited whenever it seemed safe to do so. When a nimble Burman tripped me up on the football field and the referee (another Burman) looked the other way, the crowd yelled with hideous laughter. This happened more than once. In the end the sneering yellow faces of young men that met me everywhere, the insults hooted after me when I was at a safe distance, got badly on my nerves. The young Buddhist priests were the worst of all. There were several thousands of them in the town and none of them seemed to have anything to do except stand on street corners and jeer at Europeans.

All this was perplexing and upsetting. For at that time I had already made up my mind that imperialism was an evil thing and the sooner I chucked up my job and got out of it the better. Theoretically — and secretly, of course — I was all for the Burmese and all against their oppressors, the British. As for the job I was doing, I hated it more bitterly than I can perhaps make clear. In a job like that you see the dirty work of Empire at close quarters. The wretched prisoners huddling in the stinking cages of the lock-ups, the grey, cowed faces of the long-term convicts, the scarred buttocks of the men who had been flogged with bamboos — all these oppressed me with an intolerable sense of guilt. But I could get nothing into perspective. I was young and ill-educated and I had had to think out my problems in the utter silence that is imposed on every Englishman in the East. I did not even know that the British Empire is dying, still less did I know that it is a great deal better than the younger empires that are going to supplant it. All I knew was that I was stuck between my hatred of the empire I served and my rage against the evil-spirited little beasts who tried to make my job impossible. With one part of my mind I thought of the British Raj as an unbreakable tyranny, as something clamped down, in saecula saeculorum, upon the will of prostrate peoples; with another part I thought that the greatest joy in the world would be to drive a bayonet into a Buddhist priest's guts. Feelings like these are the normal by-products of imperialism; ask any Anglo-Indian official, if you can catch him off duty.

One day something happened which in a roundabout way was enlightening. It was a tiny incident in itself, but it gave me a better glimpse than I had had before of the real nature of imperialism — the real motives for which despotic governments act. Early one morning the sub-inspector at a police station the other end of the town rang me up on the phone and said that an elephant was ravaging the bazaar. Would I please come and do something about it? I did not know what I could do, but I wanted to see what was happening and I got on to a pony and started out. I took my rifle, an old .44 Winchester and much too small to kill an elephant, but I thought the noise might be useful in terrorem. Various Burmans stopped me on the way and told me about the elephant's doings. It was not, of course, a wild elephant, but a tame one which had gone 'must'. It had been chained up, as tame elephants always are when their attack of 'must' is due, but on the previous night it had broken its chain and escaped. Its mahout, the only person who could manage it when it was in that state, had set out in pursuit, but had taken the wrong direction and was now twelve hours' journey away, and in the morning the elephant had suddenly reappeared in the town. The Burmese population had no weapons and were quite helpless against it. It had already destroyed somebody's bamboo hut, killed a cow and raided some fruit-stalls and devoured the stock; also it had met the municipal rubbish van and, when the driver jumped out and took to his heels, had turned the van over and inflicted violences upon it.

sentences.original: In Moulmein, in Lower Burma, I was hated by large numbers of people — the only time in my life that I have been important enough for this to happen to me.
sentences.translation: 하부 버마의 몰메인에서, 나는 많은 사람들에게 미움을 받았다 — 내 인생에서 이런 일이 일어날 만큼 내가 중요한 존재였던 유일한 때였다.

grammarElements.text: the only time in my life that I have been important enough for this to happen to me
grammarElements.label: 동격 삽입구 (dash 뒤 부연)

vocabulary.example: He was hated by the entire village.
Large numbers of people attended the rally.

expressions.phrase: the only time in my life
expressions.usage: 극히 드문 경험을 강조할 때

expressions.phrase: important enough for this to happen
expressions.meaning: 이런 일이 일어날 만큼 충분히 중요한
expressions.usage: enough + for + 목적어 + to부정사 구조로 조건을 설명

patterns.pattern: I was [past participle] by [agent] — the only time [clause]
patterns.explanation: 수동태로 경험을 서술한 뒤, 대시(—)를 사용하여 보충 설명을 덧붙이는 문학적 패턴.
examples: I was praised by the critics — the only time I felt my work mattered.
She was recognized by the crowd — the only moment she felt truly seen.

drillQuestions.question: I was hated _____ large numbers of people.
I have been important _____ for this to happen to me.

sentences.notes: 에세이의 첫 문장으로, 아이러니한 자기 비하(self-deprecation)로 시작한다. "중요한 존재였던 유일한 때"라는 표현은 오웰 특유의 건조한 유머. 수동태(was hated)는 자신의 무력감을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: In Moulmein, in Lower Burma
I was hated by large numbers of people
the only time in my life
that I have been important enough
for this to happen to me

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 아이러니(irony) — "미움받을 만큼 중요했다"는 자조적 표현으로 식민 관리의 모순적 위치를 암시

sentences.original: I was sub-divisional police officer of the town, and in an aimless, petty kind of way anti-European feeling was very bitter.
sentences.translation: 나는 그 마을의 지구 경찰관이었고, 목적 없이 사소한 방식으로 반유럽 감정이 매우 거셌다.

grammarElements.text: sub-divisional police officer of the town
grammarElements.label: 보어

grammarElements.text: in an aimless, petty kind of way
grammarElements.label: 양태 전치사구

vocabulary.example: He served as a sub-divisional officer in the colony.
He spent aimless days wandering the streets.
He was tired of petty arguments.
There was bitter resentment among the workers.

expressions.phrase: in an aimless, petty kind of way
expressions.usage: 감정이나 행동의 양상을 축소하거나 폄하할 때
접두사 anti-를 사용한 복합 명사

patterns.pattern: in a [adjective], [adjective] kind of way
patterns.explanation: 행동이나 감정의 양태를 묘사하는 패턴. 두 형용사를 병렬하여 뉘앙스를 더한다.
examples: In a quiet, determined kind of way, she changed the world.
In a slow, painful kind of way, the truth came out.

drillQuestions.question: Anti-European feeling was very _____.
In an aimless, _____ kind of way, the hostility grew.

sentences.notes: "aimless, petty"라는 형용사 쌍이 식민지 저항의 성격을 축소하는 효과를 만든다. 오웰은 공감과 경멸을 동시에 표현하고 있다.

koreanAlignment.en: I was sub-divisional police officer of the town
in an aimless, petty kind of way
anti-European feeling was very bitter

pronunciationNotes: petty: /ˈpeti/ — "페티", t는 미국식에서 플랩 /ɾ/로 발음 가능

sentences.original: No one had the guts to raise a riot, but if a European woman went through the bazaars alone somebody would probably spit betel juice over her dress.
sentences.translation: 아무도 폭동을 일으킬 배짱이 없었지만, 유럽인 여성이 혼자 시장을 지나가면 누군가가 아마 그녀의 옷에 빈랑 즙을 뱉었을 것이다.

grammarElements.text: the guts to raise a riot
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (명사 + to부정사)

grammarElements.text: if a European woman went through the bazaars alone
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if — 가정법 과거)

grammarElements.text: somebody would probably spit betel juice over her dress
grammarElements.label: 주절 (would — 반복적 가정)

vocabulary.example: She had the guts to speak up against the boss.
The protest turned into a riot.
The bazaar was full of colorful spices and textiles.
Chewing betel is common in Southeast Asia.

expressions.usage: 용기나 대담함이 필요한 행동을 할 때
집단적 반란이나 소요를 유발할 때

patterns.pattern: No one had the guts to [verb], but if [condition], [someone] would [verb]
patterns.explanation: 직접적 대항은 없으나 간접적 적대가 있음을 보여주는 대비 패턴.
examples: No one had the guts to complain, but if the manager left, people would mock him.
No one dared to protest openly, but if given the chance, they would resist.

drillQuestions.question: No one had the _____ to raise a riot.
If a European woman went through the bazaars, somebody _____ spit on her.

sentences.notes: "had the guts"는 비격식적 표현으로, 오웰이 문학적 산문에서도 구어체를 섞는 특유의 스타일을 보여준다. "would probably spit"의 would는 과거의 반복적 습관을 나타내는 용법.

koreanAlignment.en: No one had the guts
to raise a riot
but if a European woman went through the bazaars alone
koreanAlignment.ko: 하지만 유럽인 여성이 혼자 시장을 지나가면

koreanAlignment.en: somebody would probably spit betel juice over her dress
koreanAlignment.ko: 누군가가 아마 그녀의 옷에 빈랑 즙을 뱉었을 것이다

sentences.original: As a police officer I was an obvious target and was baited whenever it seemed safe to do so.
sentences.translation: 경찰관으로서 나는 뻔한 표적이었고, 안전해 보일 때마다 놀림을 당했다.

grammarElements.text: whenever it seemed safe to do so
grammarElements.label: 종속절 (시간 — whenever)

vocabulary.example: The answer was obvious to everyone.
He became a target for bullies.
They baited him with insults until he lost his temper.

expressions.phrase: whenever it seemed safe to do so
expressions.usage: 조건부 반복을 표현할 때 — 위험이 없을 때만 행동함을 의미
자격이나 역할을 설명하는 전치사 as

patterns.pattern: As a [role], I was [noun] and was [past participle] whenever [condition]
patterns.explanation: 역할을 명시한 뒤, 그 역할로 인해 받는 처우를 설명하는 패턴.
examples: As a newcomer, I was an outsider and was ignored whenever possible.
As a foreigner, I was a curiosity and was stared at whenever I went out.

drillQuestions.question: _____ a police officer, I was an obvious target.
I was baited _____ it seemed safe to do so.

sentences.notes: "baited"는 동물을 미끼로 유인하여 괴롭히는 행위(bear-baiting 등)에서 유래한 단어. 식민지 관리가 동물처럼 놀림당하는 상황의 굴욕감을 효과적으로 전달한다.

koreanAlignment.en: As a police officer
I was an obvious target
and was baited
whenever it seemed safe to do so

sentences.original: When a nimble Burman tripped me up on the football field and the referee (another Burman) looked the other way, the crowd yelled with hideous laughter.
sentences.translation: 민첩한 버마인이 축구장에서 나를 넘어뜨리고 심판(역시 버마인)이 모른 척했을 때, 군중은 끔찍한 웃음소리를 질렀다.

grammarElements.text: a nimble Burman tripped me up on the football field
grammarElements.label: 종속절 1

grammarElements.text: and the referee (another Burman) looked the other way
grammarElements.label: 종속절 2 (등위)

vocabulary.example: The nimble cat jumped over the fence.
He tripped me up during the race.
The referee blew the whistle for a foul.
She let out a hideous scream.

expressions.usage: 의도적으로 부정행위나 잘못을 무시할 때
악의적이고 조롱하는 웃음을 묘사할 때

patterns.pattern: When [subject A] [verb] and [subject B] [verb], [subject C] [verb]
patterns.explanation: 두 가지 동시 행동(원인)이 있고 그에 따른 결과가 오는 복합 시간절 패턴.
examples: When the power went out and the backup failed, chaos ensued.
When the speaker stumbled and the microphone cut off, the audience laughed.

drillQuestions.question: A nimble Burman _____ me up on the football field.
The referee looked the _____ way.

sentences.notes: "looked the other way"는 이중적 의미: 물리적으로 다른 곳을 봤다는 뜻과 비유적으로 모른 척했다는 뜻. 축구장이라는 공적 공간에서의 의도적 굴욕이 오웰의 분노를 잘 드러낸다.

koreanAlignment.en: When a nimble Burman tripped me up
on the football field
and the referee (another Burman) looked the other way
the crowd yelled with hideous laughter

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 구체적 일화(concrete anecdote) — 축구장 사건이라는 생생한 장면으로 적대감을 구체화

sentences.original: In the end the sneering yellow faces of young men that met me everywhere, the insults hooted after me when I was at a safe distance, got badly on my nerves.
sentences.translation: 결국 어디서나 나를 맞이하는 젊은이들의 비웃는 얼굴들, 내가 안전한 거리에 있을 때 나를 향해 외치는 모욕들이 극심하게 나의 신경을 건드렸다.

grammarElements.text: the sneering yellow faces of young men that met me everywhere
grammarElements.label: 주어 1 (관계대명사절 포함)

grammarElements.text: the insults hooted after me when I was at a safe distance
grammarElements.label: 주어 2 (병렬 — 과거분사구 수식)

vocabulary.example: He spoke in a sneering tone.
The audience hooted at the performer.
He hurled insults at his opponent.

expressions.usage: 지속적으로 짜증을 유발하는 상황을 표현할 때
보복당하지 않을 만큼 멀리서 행동할 때

patterns.pattern: [Noun phrase A], [Noun phrase B], got badly on my nerves
patterns.explanation: 복수의 긴 주어(명사구)가 나열된 뒤 하나의 동사로 귀결되는 구조. 불만이 축적되는 효과.
examples: The constant noise, the cramped space, the lack of privacy, got badly on my nerves.

drillQuestions.question: The sneering faces got badly _____ my nerves.
The insults were _____ after me at a safe distance.

sentences.notes: 매우 긴 주어가 두 개 병렬(faces + insults)되어 동사(got)가 문장 끝 가까이에 온다. 이 지연된 동사 구조가 분노가 축적되는 심리적 효과를 만든다. "yellow faces"는 당시 시대적 표현.

koreanAlignment.en: In the end
the sneering yellow faces of young men
that met me everywhere
the insults hooted after me
when I was at a safe distance
got badly on my nerves

sentences.original: The young Buddhist priests were the worst of all.
sentences.translation: 젊은 불교 승려들이 그중 가장 나빴다.

vocabulary.example: The Buddhist temple was centuries old.
The priest conducted the ceremony.

expressions.usage: 여러 항목 중 최악을 지적할 때 사용하는 최상급 표현

patterns.pattern: [Subject] were the worst of all.
patterns.explanation: 짧고 단정적인 문장으로 최악의 대상을 지목하는 패턴. 앞선 긴 서술 후에 간결한 판단을 내림.
examples: The bureaucrats were the worst of all.
Monday mornings were the worst of all.

drillQuestions.question: The young Buddhist priests were the _____ of all.

sentences.notes: 이전의 긴 복합문 뒤에 오는 극도로 짧은 문장. 이 대비가 강한 임팩트를 만든다. 오웰의 문체적 특징인 "긴 문장 → 짧은 문장"의 리듬감을 보여주는 전형적 예.

koreanAlignment.en: The young Buddhist priests
were the worst of all

sentences.original: There were several thousands of them in the town and none of them seemed to have anything to do except stand on street corners and jeer at Europeans.
sentences.translation: 마을에 수천 명이 있었고 그들 중 누구도 거리 모퉁이에 서서 유럽인들을 야유하는 것 외에는 할 일이 없어 보였다.

grammarElements.text: seemed to have anything to do
grammarElements.label: 동사 (seem + to부정사)

grammarElements.text: except stand on street corners and jeer at Europeans
grammarElements.label: 예외구 (except + 동사원형 병렬)

vocabulary.example: The crowd jeered at the losing team.
They gathered on the street corner.

expressions.usage: 무위도식하는 상태를 비판적으로 묘사할 때
경멸이나 조롱을 담아 소리치는 행위

patterns.pattern: None of them seemed to have anything to do except [verb]
patterns.explanation: 집단의 무위도식함을 비꼬는 패턴. except 뒤에 유일한 활동을 나열하여 풍자 효과.
examples: None of them seemed to have anything to do except complain about the weather.
None of them seemed to have anything to do except gossip about neighbors.

drillQuestions.question: None of them seemed to have anything to do _____ jeer at Europeans.
They would stand on street corners and _____ at Europeans.

sentences.notes: "several thousands"는 문법적으로 "several thousand"이 표준이지만, 오웰은 과장을 위해 의도적으로 -s를 붙였을 가능성. except 뒤에 to 없이 바로 동사원형(stand)이 오는 구조에 주목.

koreanAlignment.en: There were several thousands of them in the town
none of them seemed to have anything to do
koreanAlignment.ko: 그들 중 누구도 할 일이 없어 보였다

koreanAlignment.en: except stand on street corners
and jeer at Europeans

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 풍자(satire) — 승려들의 행동을 "거리에서 야유하기"로 축약하여 냉소적으로 묘사

sentences.original: All this was perplexing and upsetting.
sentences.translation: 이 모든 것이 당혹스럽고 속상했다.

vocabulary.example: The results were perplexing to the scientists.
The news was deeply upsetting.

expressions.usage: 앞서 서술한 여러 상황을 한꺼번에 가리킬 때

patterns.pattern: All this was [adjective] and [adjective].
patterns.explanation: 앞 단락의 복잡한 서술을 짧게 요약하며 새 단락을 여는 전환 패턴.
examples: All this was confusing and exhausting.
All this was inspiring and terrifying.

drillQuestions.question: All this was perplexing and _____.

sentences.notes: 새로운 단락의 시작. 짧은 문장으로 앞 단락 전체를 요약하며, 내면의 갈등을 암시하는 전환 문장. perplexing(지적 혼란)과 upsetting(감정적 동요)의 병렬이 이성과 감정의 이중 고민을 표현.

koreanAlignment.en: All this
was perplexing and upsetting

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 전환(transition) — 짧은 감정 요약으로 외적 서술에서 내적 갈등으로 전환

sentences.original: For at that time I had already made up my mind that imperialism was an evil thing and the sooner I chucked up my job and got out of it the better.
sentences.translation: 왜냐하면 그 당시 나는 이미 제국주의가 사악한 것이며, 하루빨리 이 일을 집어치우고 벗어나는 것이 낫다고 마음을 정했기 때문이다.

grammarElements.text: had already made up my mind
grammarElements.label: 동사 (과거완료)

grammarElements.text: that imperialism was an evil thing
grammarElements.label: that절 1 (목적어절)

grammarElements.text: and the sooner I chucked up my job and got out of it the better
grammarElements.label: the 비교급 ~ the 비교급 구문

vocabulary.example: Imperialism shaped the modern world.
He chucked up his job and moved abroad.
The dictator committed evil acts.

expressions.usage: 중요한 결정을 내릴 때
행동의 긴급성을 강조할 때 사용하는 비교 구문

patterns.pattern: The sooner [subject] [verb], the better
patterns.explanation: "the 비교급 ~ the 비교급" 구문. 하나의 변화가 다른 결과를 비례적으로 향상시킴을 표현.
examples: The sooner we leave, the better.
The sooner you start studying, the better your results will be.

drillQuestions.question: I had already made _____ my mind.
The _____ I chucked up my job, the better.

sentences.notes: "For"이 문장 첫머리에서 이유를 나타내는 접속사로 쓰인 것은 문어체 특징. "chuck up"은 매우 구어적 표현으로, 격식적 서술 속에 비격식 표현을 섞는 오웰의 특유 스타일.

koreanAlignment.en: For at that time
I had already made up my mind
that imperialism was an evil thing
and the sooner I chucked up my job
and got out of it the better

sentences.original: Theoretically — and secretly, of course — I was all for the Burmese and all against their oppressors, the British.
sentences.translation: 이론적으로 — 그리고 물론 비밀리에 — 나는 전적으로 버마인 편이었고, 그들의 억압자인 영국인에 전적으로 반대했다.

grammarElements.text: and all against their oppressors, the British
grammarElements.label: 보어 2 (동격 포함)

vocabulary.example: Theoretically, the plan should work.
The people rose up against their oppressors.
She secretly admired his courage.

expressions.usage: 강한 지지를 비격식적으로 표현할 때
all for의 반대 표현으로 대비 효과

patterns.pattern: I was all for [X] and all against [Y]
patterns.explanation: "all for"와 "all against"의 대비 구조로 입장을 명확히 하는 패턴.
examples: I was all for reform and all against the status quo.
She was all for adventure and all against routine.

drillQuestions.question: I was all _____ the Burmese.
I was all _____ their oppressors.

sentences.notes: "theoretically — and secretly"의 삽입구는 공적 입장과 사적 감정의 괴리를 드러낸다. 대시(—)로 둘러싸인 삽입은 오웰의 전형적인 문체적 장치. "the British"가 "oppressors"의 동격으로, 자국민을 억압자로 규정하는 강한 비판.

koreanAlignment.en: Theoretically
and secretly, of course
I was all for the Burmese
and all against their oppressors, the British

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 삽입구(parenthetical) + 대비(antithesis) — "theoretically/secretly"와 "for/against"의 이중 대비로 내면의 모순을 강조

sentences.original: As for the job I was doing, I hated it more bitterly than I can perhaps make clear.
sentences.translation: 내가 하고 있는 일에 대해 말하자면, 아마 명확히 표현할 수 없을 만큼 더 격렬하게 그것을 혐오했다.

grammarElements.text: As for the job I was doing
grammarElements.label: 화제 전환 전치사구 (as for)

grammarElements.text: more bitterly than I can perhaps make clear
grammarElements.label: 비교급 부사구 (than절)

vocabulary.example: She cried bitterly when she heard the news.
Let me make my position clear.

expressions.usage: 새로운 화제를 도입하거나 특정 대상에 대해 언급할 때

expressions.phrase: more ... than I can make clear
expressions.meaning: 명확히 표현할 수 없을 만큼 더
expressions.usage: 언어로 표현하기 어려운 강렬한 감정을 나타낼 때

patterns.pattern: I [verb] it more [adverb] than I can perhaps [verb]
patterns.explanation: 감정의 깊이가 표현 능력을 초과함을 나타내는 패턴. 문학적 겸양.
examples: I loved her more deeply than I can perhaps explain.
I feared the outcome more intensely than I can describe.

drillQuestions.question: As _____ the job I was doing, I hated it.
I hated it more bitterly _____ I can make clear.

sentences.notes: "more bitterly than I can perhaps make clear"는 말로 표현할 수 없는 감정의 깊이를 인정하는 표현. "perhaps"의 삽입이 오웰 특유의 신중한 어조를 보여준다.

koreanAlignment.en: As for the job I was doing
koreanAlignment.ko: 내가 하고 있는 일에 대해 말하자면

koreanAlignment.en: I hated it more bitterly
than I can perhaps make clear
koreanAlignment.ko: 아마 명확히 표현할 수 없을 만큼

sentences.original: In a job like that you see the dirty work of Empire at close quarters.
sentences.translation: 그런 직업을 하면 제국의 더러운 일을 가까이에서 보게 된다.

vocabulary.example: Someone has to do the dirty work.
The British Empire spanned the globe.
He observed the battle at close quarters.

expressions.usage: 식민 통치의 폭력적이고 비윤리적인 실무를 가리킬 때
거리감 없이 직접 경험하거나 목격할 때

patterns.pattern: In a [noun] like that, you [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: "일반적 you"를 사용하여 특정 경험을 보편적 진실로 확장하는 패턴.
examples: In a war like that, you lose your innocence.
In a city like that, you learn to be careful.

drillQuestions.question: You see the dirty work of Empire at close _____.
In a job _____ that, you see terrible things.

sentences.notes: "you"는 화자 자신이 아닌 "일반적 you"(generic you)로, 독자를 끌어들여 경험을 공유하는 효과. "Empire"의 대문자 사용이 영국 제국을 하나의 고유명사처럼 다루며, "dirty work"라는 비격식 표현과의 대비가 식민지 현실의 추악함을 강조.

koreanAlignment.en: In a job like that
you see
the dirty work of Empire
at close quarters

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 직접 호소(direct address) — "you"로 독자를 끌어들여 식민지 현실에 직면하게 함

sentences.original: The wretched prisoners huddling in the stinking cages of the lock-ups, the grey, cowed faces of the long-term convicts, the scarred buttocks of the men who had been flogged with bamboos — all these oppressed me with an intolerable sense of guilt.
sentences.translation: 감옥의 악취 나는 우리에서 웅크리고 있는 비참한 수감자들, 장기 수형자들의 잿빛 겁에 질린 얼굴들, 대나무로 매를 맞아 흉터가 남은 사람들의 엉덩이 — 이 모든 것이 참을 수 없는 죄책감으로 나를 짓눌렀다.

grammarElements.text: The wretched prisoners huddling in the stinking cages of the lock-ups
grammarElements.label: 주어 1 (현재분사구 후치수식)

grammarElements.text: the grey, cowed faces of the long-term convicts
grammarElements.label: 주어 2 (병렬)

grammarElements.text: the scarred buttocks of the men who had been flogged with bamboos
grammarElements.label: 주어 3 (관계대명사절 포함)

grammarElements.text: with an intolerable sense of guilt
grammarElements.label: 양태 전치사구

vocabulary.example: The refugees lived in wretched conditions.
They huddled together for warmth.
The stinking rubbish filled the alley.
The cowed workers dared not speak.
The convicts were sent to a penal colony.
Prisoners were flogged for minor offenses.
The heat was intolerable.

expressions.usage: 감정적 압박을 표현할 때. oppress는 물리적·심리적 억압 모두에 사용
극도의 심리적 고통을 형용할 때

patterns.pattern: [Noun A], [Noun B], [Noun C] — all these [verb] me with [emotion]
patterns.explanation: 세 개의 구체적 이미지를 나열한 뒤 "all these"로 요약하고 감정적 반응을 서술하는 패턴. 강렬한 수사학적 나열(tricolon).
examples: The empty streets, the boarded windows, the silence — all these filled me with dread.

drillQuestions.question: All these oppressed me _____ an intolerable sense of guilt.
The men had been _____ with bamboos.

sentences.notes: 오웰 산문의 가장 유명한 문장 중 하나. 세 개의 구체적 이미지(수감자/얼굴/엉덩이)가 점점 더 육체적이고 충격적으로 escalate된다. 삼단 열거(tricolon) + 점층법(climax)의 결합. "oppressed"가 식민지 억압(oppression)을 화자 자신에게 되돌리는 아이러니.

koreanAlignment.en: The wretched prisoners huddling in the stinking cages of the lock-ups
koreanAlignment.ko: 감옥의 악취 나는 우리에서 웅크리고 있는 비참한 수감자들

koreanAlignment.en: the grey, cowed faces of the long-term convicts
koreanAlignment.ko: 장기 수형자들의 잿빛 겁에 질린 얼굴들

koreanAlignment.en: the scarred buttocks of the men who had been flogged with bamboos
koreanAlignment.ko: 대나무로 매를 맞아 흉터가 남은 사람들의 엉덩이

koreanAlignment.en: all these oppressed me
with an intolerable sense of guilt

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 삼단 열거(tricolon) + 점층법(climax) — 세 이미지가 점점 더 적나라해지며 정서적 강도를 높임

sentences.original: But I could get nothing into perspective.
sentences.translation: 하지만 나는 아무것도 올바른 관점으로 바라볼 수 없었다.

vocabulary.example: Try to see things from a different perspective.

expressions.usage: 혼란스러운 상황을 냉정하게 판단하려 할 때

patterns.pattern: I could get nothing into perspective
patterns.explanation: "could + 부정어(nothing)"로 무능력을 표현. "get into perspective"는 사물을 합리적으로 판단하는 것.
examples: I could make nothing of the situation.
She could put nothing into words.

drillQuestions.question: I could get nothing into _____.

sentences.notes: 다시 짧은 문장으로 전환. 앞의 긴 감정적 서술 뒤에 오는 간결한 고백. "nothing"의 절대적 부정이 내면의 혼란 깊이를 보여준다.

koreanAlignment.en: But I could get nothing
into perspective

sentences.original: I was young and ill-educated and I had had to think out my problems in the utter silence that is imposed on every Englishman in the East.
sentences.translation: 나는 젊고 교육을 제대로 받지 못했으며, 동양의 모든 영국인에게 부과되는 완전한 침묵 속에서 나의 문제들을 스스로 생각해내야 했다.

grammarElements.text: and I had had to think out my problems
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (과거완료 — had had to)

grammarElements.text: that is imposed on every Englishman in the East
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (that — silence를 수식)

vocabulary.example: He felt ill-educated compared to his peers.
I had to think out the problem on my own.
The room was in utter silence.
The government imposed new restrictions.

expressions.usage: 고립된 상태에서 아무런 소통 없이
과거의 특정 시점 이전에 의무가 있었음을 표현

patterns.pattern: I was [adjective] and [adjective] and I had had to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 자신의 부족함을 인정한 뒤, 과거완료로 그 상황에서 해야 했던 행동을 서술하는 패턴.
examples: I was naive and inexperienced and I had had to learn everything from scratch.

drillQuestions.question: I had _____ to think out my problems alone.
The utter silence is _____ on every Englishman in the East.

sentences.notes: "had had to"는 과거완료(had) + have to의 과거분사(had to)의 결합으로, 문법적으로 까다롭다. "the utter silence imposed on every Englishman"은 식민지 영국인들 사이의 감정 표현 금기를 가리키며, 이것이 자기 인식을 방해했다는 오웰의 분석.

koreanAlignment.en: I was young and ill-educated
koreanAlignment.ko: 나는 젊고 교육을 제대로 받지 못했으며

koreanAlignment.en: and I had had to think out my problems
in the utter silence
that is imposed on every Englishman in the East

sentences.original: One day something happened which in a roundabout way was enlightening.
sentences.translation: 어느 날, 우회적인 방식으로 깨달음을 주는 일이 일어났다.

grammarElements.text: which in a roundabout way was enlightening
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (which — something을 수식)

vocabulary.example: He took a roundabout way to explain his point.
The lecture was truly enlightening.

expressions.usage: 직접적이지 않은 방식으로 무언가를 알게 될 때
과거의 특정하지 않은 날을 이야기할 때 — 서사의 시작 신호

patterns.pattern: One day something happened which [clause]
patterns.explanation: 이야기의 핵심 사건을 도입하는 전형적 서사 패턴. "one day"로 시간을 설정하고 관계절로 사건의 의미를 부여.
examples: One day something happened which changed my life forever.
One day something occurred which I could never forget.

drillQuestions.question: Something happened which in a _____ way was enlightening.
One day something happened _____ was enlightening.

sentences.notes: 에세이의 구조적 전환점. 앞의 두 단락이 배경(setting)이었다면, 이 문장부터 본격적인 사건(plot)이 시작된다. "roundabout"이라는 단어가 사건의 의미가 즉각적이지 않고 성찰을 통해 드러남을 암시.

koreanAlignment.en: One day
something happened
which in a roundabout way
was enlightening

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 서사 전환(narrative shift) — 배경 서술에서 핵심 사건으로의 전환, 독자의 기대를 환기

sentences.original: It was a tiny incident in itself, but it gave me a better glimpse than I had had before of the real nature of imperialism — the real motives for which despotic governments act.
sentences.translation: 그 자체로는 사소한 사건이었지만, 제국주의의 진정한 본질 — 전제 정부가 행동하는 진정한 동기 — 에 대해 이전보다 더 나은 통찰을 주었다.

grammarElements.text: a better glimpse than I had had before
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (비교급)

grammarElements.text: of the real nature of imperialism
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (대상)

grammarElements.text: — the real motives for which despotic governments act
grammarElements.label: 동격 삽입구 (대시 뒤 — 관계대명사 for which)

vocabulary.example: I caught a glimpse of the truth.
The despotic ruler silenced all opposition.
The police investigated the motive for the crime.

expressions.usage: 어떤 것의 본질적 중요성을 축소하거나 한정할 때
표면 아래 숨겨진 진짜 성격을 드러낼 때

patterns.pattern: It was a [small thing] in itself, but it gave me [a better understanding] of [something larger]
patterns.explanation: 작은 사건이 큰 깨달음으로 이어지는 구조. 에세이 문학의 전형적 도입부.
examples: It was a simple conversation in itself, but it gave me a deeper understanding of her pain.

drillQuestions.question: It gave me a better _____ of the real nature of imperialism.
The real motives _____ which despotic governments act.

sentences.notes: "for which despotic governments act"에서 "for which"는 "the motives for which"의 관계대명사 구조. 전치사(for)가 관계대명사(which) 앞에 오는 격식체. "had had before"는 과거완료의 반복으로 시간의 선후 관계를 명확히 한다.

koreanAlignment.en: It was a tiny incident in itself
but it gave me a better glimpse
than I had had before
of the real nature of imperialism
the real motives for which despotic governments act

sentences.original: Early one morning the sub-inspector at a police station the other end of the town rang me up on the phone and said that an elephant was ravaging the bazaar.
sentences.translation: 어느 이른 아침, 마을 반대편 경찰서의 부감독관이 전화를 걸어 코끼리 한 마리가 시장을 짓밟고 있다고 말했다.

grammarElements.text: the sub-inspector at a police station the other end of the town
grammarElements.label: 주어 (장소 수식 포함)

grammarElements.text: rang me up on the phone
grammarElements.label: 동사 (구동사 과거)

grammarElements.text: that an elephant was ravaging the bazaar
grammarElements.label: that절 (간접화법)

vocabulary.example: The sub-inspector filed the report.
She rang me up to tell me the news.
The storm ravaged the coastline.

expressions.phrase: rang me up on the phone
expressions.usage: 영국 영어에서 전화 통화를 시작할 때. ring up은 미국 영어의 call에 해당
이야기의 구체적 시간을 설정하는 서사적 시작

patterns.pattern: Early one [time], [someone] [verb] and said that [event]
patterns.explanation: 사건의 시작을 보고하는 서사 패턴. 시간 + 인물 + 보고 행위 + 사건 내용.
examples: Early one evening, a stranger arrived and said that trouble was coming.
Late one night, the guard called and said that someone had broken in.

drillQuestions.question: The sub-inspector _____ me up on the phone.
An elephant was _____ the bazaar.

sentences.notes: "ring up"은 영국 영어 특유의 표현으로, 미국 영어의 "call"에 해당. "the other end of the town"에서 관사가 없는 것은 오웰 시대 문체의 특징. "ravaging"의 진행형이 사건의 긴급성을 전달.

koreanAlignment.en: Early one morning
the sub-inspector at a police station the other end of the town
rang me up on the phone
and said that an elephant was ravaging the bazaar
koreanAlignment.ko: 코끼리 한 마리가 시장을 짓밟고 있다고 말했다

sentences.original: I did not know what I could do, but I wanted to see what was happening and I got on to a pony and started out.
sentences.translation: 내가 무엇을 할 수 있을지 몰랐지만, 무슨 일이 벌어지고 있는지 보고 싶었고, 조랑말에 올라타 출발했다.

grammarElements.text: and I got on to a pony and started out
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (연속 행동)

vocabulary.example: He rode a pony through the countryside.
We started out early in the morning.

expressions.phrase: did not know what I could do
expressions.usage: 불확실한 상황에서의 무력감을 표현
영국 영어에서 탈것에 올라탈 때 (미국식은 got on)

patterns.pattern: I did not know [what clause], but I wanted to [verb] and I [verb]
patterns.explanation: 무지/불확실 속에서도 행동하는 것을 표현하는 패턴. 주저하면서도 움직이는 심리.
examples: I did not know what to expect, but I wanted to find out and I went ahead.
I did not know what I was getting into, but I wanted to help and I volunteered.

drillQuestions.question: I did not know _____ I could do.
I got on _____ a pony and started out.

sentences.notes: 세 개의 등위절(did not know / wanted / got on)이 연결되며 화자의 행동 과정을 보여준다. "did not know"로 시작하지만 "but"으로 행동으로 전환하는 것이, 의무감에 이끌려 움직이는 식민 관리의 상황을 반영.

koreanAlignment.en: I did not know what I could do
koreanAlignment.ko: 내가 무엇을 할 수 있을지 몰랐지만

koreanAlignment.en: but I wanted to see what was happening
koreanAlignment.ko: 무슨 일이 벌어지고 있는지 보고 싶었고

koreanAlignment.en: and I got on to a pony and started out
